preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to the voiced dental fricative [ð] is a multi-stage lenition process. It typically involves an intermediate voiced stop stage [d], which then spirantizes in intervocalic positions to allow continuous airflow. This shift is a key historical link in Germanic and Romance phonology.

examples.note: Intervocalic lenition where the stop voiced and then spirantized into a fricative/approximant.
Historical shift of final stops to 'soft' voiced fricatives.
A later English shift where voiced stops shifted to fricatives before historical r-suffixes.

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
